title: A Derry On A Cove
content:
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Twas in the felonï؟½s dock he stood, his eyes were black and blue;
آ آ آ آ His voice with grief was broken, and his nose was broken, too;
آ آ آ آ He muttered, as that broken nose he wiped upon his cap,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Itï؟½s orfal when the pï؟½leece has got a derry on a chap.

آ آ آ آ ï؟½I am a honest workinï؟½ cove, as any bloke can see,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Itï؟½s just because the pï؟½leece has got a derry, sir, on me;
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Oh, yes, the legal gents can grin, I say it ainï؟½t no joke,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Itï؟½s cruel when the pï؟½leece has got a derry on a bloke.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ ï؟½Why donï؟½t you go to work?ï؟½ he said (he muttered, ï؟½Why donï؟½t you?ï؟½).
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Yer honer knows as well as me there ainï؟½t no work to do.
آ آ آ آ ï؟½And when I try to find a job Iï؟½m shaddered by a trap,
آ آ آ آ ï؟½Itï؟½s awful when the pï؟½leece has got a derry on a chap.ï؟½

آ آ آ آ I sighï؟½d and shed a tearlet for that noble nature marred,
آ آ آ آ But, ah! the Bench was rough on him, and gave him six monthsï؟½ hard.
آ آ آ آ He only said, ï؟½Beyond the grave youï؟½ll cop it hot, by Jove!
آ آ آ آ ï؟½There ainï؟½t no angel pï؟½leece to get a derry on a cove.ï؟½
meaning: About: Henry Lawson (17 June 1867 - 2 September 1922) was an Australian writer and poet. Along with his contemporary Banjo Paterson, Lawson is among the best-known Australian poets and fiction writers of the colonial period.
